summary: DHH reviews the Framework Desktop, a tiny 4.5L silent PC powered by AMD's Ryzen AI Max 395+ chip. He finds it crushes multi-core workloads like running the HEY test suite, beating both competing mini PCs and Apple's M4 Max by significant margins. While acknowledging Apple's single-core advantage, he argues multi-core performance is what developers actually feel in daily work. At roughly half the price of a comparable Mac Studio, he considers it an exceptional value for Linux developers, though he notes the cheaper Beelink SER9 remains a solid alternative for those less sensitive to multi-core speed.
key_points: The Framework Desktop with AMD 395+ is nearly twice as fast as the Beelink SER8 and 40-50% faster than M4 Max/Pro in multi-core developer workloads
At $1,876 for 64GB/2TB, it costs nearly half the $3,299 Mac Studio with similar specs while outperforming it for Docker-based development
Single-core performance differences between modern machines are imperceptible in daily use — multi-core workloads are where you actually feel the wait
The completely silent operation is a dealbreaker feature that separates it from cheaper alternatives like GMKTec
Local LLM capability with 128GB unified memory is impressive but frontier SaaS models still far outperform what runs locally
money_quotes: It's completely quiet, even under heavy, stress-all-cores load.
I can't listen to roaring fans anymore.
Docker is an integral part of the workflow for tons of developers. So this is what Real World benchmarks reveal.
I've found that all these computers feel fast enough in single-core performance these days.
The only time I actually feel like I'm waiting on anything is in multi-core workloads like the HEY test suite.
To get a Mac Studio with similar specs you'll literally spend nearly twice as much at $3,299!
I want the best models running at full speed, and right now that means SaaS.
What a great time to love open source software and tinker-friendly hardware!
key_insight: The Framework Desktop delivers superior multi-core developer performance at half the price of a Mac Studio, making Linux on tinker-friendly hardware the best value proposition for developers today.
